sentences.text: My name is Erna Florsheim.
I spell it F-L-O-R-S-H-E-I-M.
My name at birth was Erna Wassermann, W-A-S-S-E-R-M-A-N-N.
Yes, I did.
My name was Elise Wilfert, W-I-L-F-E-R-T.
No.
Sometimes she called me Erenele.
I was born November 27, 1926.
In Konigsbach, Baden, Germany.
It is in southern Germany.
The-- on one direction of Konigsbach is Pforzheim, P-F-O-R-Z-H-E-I-M.
On the other direction is Karlsruhe, K-A-R-L-S-R-U-H-E.
Approximately a half an hour by train.
Approximately.
It was a small town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no industry.
A lot of farming was around that area.
And it was peaceful at one time.
Wheat and vegetables, fruits, and so on.
We lived in a house that was my grandparents' home.
And when my mother got married to my father, he-- they lived there.
And the four of us were born there, my sister and two brothers and I.
My father was-- I believe when he started out a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a cattle [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] merchant.
And later, some years later, he was in business with my uncle in Pforzheim and he worked there.
He was-- my uncle had a business of building material.
And my father coordinated and helped in various capacities.
No.
I never knew my grandparents.
They had died many years before.
It-- my grandparents left the house to my mother.
And this is why we lived there.
And we never moved.
My sister Ilse is the oldest.
And she is approximately six years older than I am.
She lives now in Florida, in Miami Beach.
Is Julius, my brother, who lives now in Seattle, Washington.
And he is about five years older than I am.
And then is me-- comes I.
And my youngest brother, Siegie, who lives in Canada.
He has two sons and one daughter.
He is five years younger than I am.
Yes, I was.
All four of us, yes.
Yes, we did.
Yes.
Yes, I did.
And my brothers shared a room together.
And it was a two-family house, actually.
We lived upstairs.
And downstairs, for a time, my uncle and aunt and their children lived there, until they immigrated to Argentina.
Yes.
Yes, on the first floor.
They lived there.
And it was a comfortable house.
It had an attic.
And it also-- we had a big backyard.
And there was a-- since my grandfather was also dealing with cattle, there was a-- a barn.
And that barn, as you walked through, it led through a very nice big garden.
In the garden, we had a few plum trees.
We had berries and grapes.
And my oldest brother, he used to plant lettuce and tomatoes and things like this, and especially parsley, soup greens that my mother used to use fresh whenever she made something.
Yes.
Yes.
She-- not too much.
She was so busy with four children.
And-- although we did have some help.
But as time went on, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish people were not allowed to have help, non-Jewish help.
And I remember my mother was sick quite often.
She had-- they did not know what it was she had.
They-- many mornings I got up and I went to her bedroom and she wasn't there and then they took her to the hospital at night for-- she was in pain.
Much later they found out that she had ulcers.
And in those days, they did not have the sophisticated X-rays and so on.
So she was not too well most of the time that I remember.
And then once they found out what was wrong, they operated on my mother.
And after that, she was perfectly healthy.
She was fine.
And so it's-- and she could take care of us and everything.
I don't remember exactly.
I was quite young.
I did go to school already, maybe the first or second grade.
But I remember my father, and then we had some help to get us ready for school.
And-- but I was very young.
It was domestic, to help with the cleaning, the washing.
My mother did the cooking whenever she was well.
She didn't want anyone in her kitchen.
And she was very particular.
She was kosher and everything-- she was always afraid that nothing gets mixed up.
For some time, non-Jewish, until they were not allowed to work in Jewish homes anymore.
And then for a while, we had some Jewish help, but not for very long, because people immigrated.
And soon after, we had no one.
And then my sister and I helped along a little bit with chores around the house.
Oh, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- for example, where we lived in Konigsbach, we could not go to school in that town for very long.
And we had to go to Pforzheim.
My father took us there by train every morning.
He wouldn't let us go alone.
He was afraid that they would stone us on the way to the train.
My father worked for-- with my uncle and for my uncle until he was not allowed to have a business anymore, because Jews just could not sell to non-Jews and so on.
So we were affected very greatly by these things.
I remember a neighbor, a non-Jewish woman.
And she used to bring vegetables and fruits and put it in behind the house for my mother to get, because-- so we had something to eat.
We could not go to the regular market to buy whatever our needs were.
Kosher meat was just about impossible to get.
It was imported from South America or Holland.
It came frozen and we received very little.
And my mother kept that for my father and for my oldest brother, because she felt he was growing up and he needed the strength.
My sister, [?
Siegie ?], and I, we were mostly brought up on dairy.
And it was difficult in every way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just-- we were-- I never went to a movie until I came to England after the war.
We had no luxuries.
Right.
They would not permit Jewish children to continue schooling there.
They told my parents they regret very much, but this is the law.
It was in the '30s, must have been '34, '35.
Just a few-- a few years that I went to public school.
And-- although they-- the teachers tried to be very nice and understanding.
But there was one teacher in particular that stands out in my mind, that was-- made me say heil Hitler every time I met him in the street.
He knew I was Jewish.
And-- but he-- I even remember his name, and I don't remember most of the others.
Kraus, K-R-A-U-S.
I don't remember his first name, but I remember it was him who always-- and if I tried to avoid passing him, he made me stand up in class the following day and reprimanded me.
So I was really not unhappy when I had to go to Pforzheim to school.
I don't-- I don't even remember my schoolmates in Konigsbach.
One writes to me now, since last year, and I have no idea who she is.
No.
He lived a different direction away from us.
He lived-- it was not very far from the train station.
And we lived a little bit away from there, not too far.
But it was within walking distance.
How would-- what could he do to make you say heil Hitler when you would meet in the street?
Just to look at me, that's all.
And this was-- he wanted this as a greeting to him.
To-- to humiliate me, I suppose, because although-- I was very young, but as the years went by and I thought about it and this is I presumed the purpose of his-- his treatment.
It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not easy, because there were already the Hitler years and we lived always in fear.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't-- I never had a childhood, because there was always the news, the speeches about Hitler.
I didn't understand what it was all about, but I could feel the tension.
And we couldn't go out to play with other children or ride a bicycle the way we would have liked to do.
So my youngest brother Siegie and I, we were mostly in our garden playing there with mud, making mud pies and things like this.

Or my mother used to give us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a cup, two cups, one with sugar in it and one with water in it so we could wash the berries first and put them then in sugar, so we-- for the treat, as a treat for us.
And we had no playmates, except the few Jewish people that were still living there.
But more and more left.
And at the end, there was just Siegie and I.
And because-- either they moved to Pforzheim or they immigrated to different parts of the world.
My sister was in Hamburg.
And she was training there as some profession and I don't even recall anymore what it was.
I know she was training for something.
My brother Julius was in a town, Geislingen it was called, I remember, in training also for some profession.
I don't know what it was.
And as a matter of fact, in November of 1938, Kristallnacht, they took my brother Julius, who was 16 years old at the time, to Dachau.
[CRYING] [SIGHS] He-- he was there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about two months.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they let him go under the condition that he leaves the country.
So my-- we had an uncle still living in Belgium, which is a brother of my mother.
And he had a daughter living in Manchester, England.
So my parents got in touch with my uncle, who got in touch with his daughter in order to get papers for my brother to leave.
And he did.
And [CRYING] soon after, my sister left for England.
And she did there domestic work.
My cousin also provided the papers.
And she got a family in Manchester where my sister could go to work for them.
So this way, the two got out earlier.
And I-- I could have gone to England [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING] But I didn't want to leave my parents alone, so I stayed at home.
And my youngest brother Siegie, he left in '39.
He was seven years old.
And he didn't even realize that-- I mean, he knew he was going to England.
And my parents told him that he was going to see my sister and my-- and my brother Julius.

And so-- as he told me not so long ago that when my father took him to the train and he went with the Kindertransport to England, he never realized that-- he thought it was just a train ride with my father, never realizing that he would not see his parents again.
[CRYING]
Yes.
Yes, I did.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to school that-- to Pforzheim, Siegie and I with my father, who always took us every day.
And he-- we went as-- when we went to the school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were sent home, because in Pforzheim, the-- by the time we came to school, the synagogues were burning already.
But we returned home.
But my father, my father went to the business.
And-- but Siegie and I went home.
And as we arrived into Konigsbach-- and the-- the train station was a little bit elevated.
And we looked to our right.
We saw the-- the synagogue burning.
And we-- and as we walked down the hill, we saw the Torahs on the ground.
We went home.
[SOBBING] And my mother was standing at the window, waiting for-- looking out for-- to see if we are-- that we got home.
She knew what was going on.
And we came to the house.
And my mother, she didn't know what to do, where to take us for safety, because they-- the few families that were still in Konigsbach, the Jewish families, they destroyed the homes completely.
And one woman that-- one woman that lived near, very near to the synagogue, she wrapped the-- a piece of the Torah and took it in.
And my mother said to me and to my-- and to Siegie-- there was a family living on the first floor, a non-Jewish family.
And she said to me, go get the keys.
Maybe we can go to their apartments to hide.
Well, I-- and she told me where to go.
Siegie and I-- I took him by the hand and we went together.
But I couldn't walk.
I-- the-- the shock from it all all was in my-- my legs.
So I returned and I told my mother, I can't go.
I cannot walk.
So what she did, she took us into the garden [INAUDIBLE].
It was in-- it was November.
It was cool already.
And we-- the clothes we had, the warm clothes.
And she took us in the garden and we stayed there until it was night.
Every once in a while, she said to me, go and look and see from the barn where the entrance is if you see something.
And-- and I couldn't see anything.
And for some reason, it's like a miracle, they never came to our house to destroy.
And then when-- at night, we went back to the house.
And the Gestapo came several times looking for my father, because they wanted to take him also to Dachau or to one of those places.
And we didn't-- for a few days, we didn't know what happened to my father, because he was in Pforzheim.
And then we heard through my uncle that he took a train, put on very old clothes, and went to his mother in Wittenberg, in-- that was Aufhausen, a small place where he came from.
And he stayed with my grandmother for a number of weeks.
But we knew he was safe and he was hiding there at my grandmother-- grandmother's.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then when he came home-- and then of course, we found out that, as I mentioned before, that Julius was in Dachau.
And we had to wait until they released him.
And he-- Julius told us horror stories from Dachau, what transpired there and the people that were tortured.
And we had-- he was lucky that they let him go and that we could-- that my parents could arrange for him to leave the country.
No.
From neighbors, non-Jewish neighbors.
Whenever Hitler spoke, my father used to go to the back and they made it loud enough for my father to listen to, to hear, and-- because we not-- we're not allowed to have any radios.
And this is how we knew what was going on.
And for a long time, my parents didn't want to realize or didn't want to make arrangements to leave the country.
My father always thought since he fought in the First World War, and nothing can happen to him.
And they were afraid with four small children to go to a new country not knowing the language.
How does one make a living?
By the time they decided that-- to want-- that they wanted to leave, it was too late.
They did apply for papers.
And they had interviews to go to-- to go to Australia.
But we never-- it was too late.
They were very kind.
They-- I must say my parents were very well-liked in the community, in the town.
And I have a feeling that this is why they never came to our house either to destroy everything.
And many times I have heard that my grandparents were very honest and honorable people.
And it was I suppose the background and that-- and just luck.
And-- but they-- as I said, that one lady who always brought us fresh vegetables and fruit.
So we-- my parents and family were very well-liked in that area, in that small town.
I don't believe so.
Not that I remember.
